questions.prompt: You receive a fire FNOL on an HO-3 policy with Endorsement 4A. The insured reports 'popping sounds and flickering lights before smoke,' and policy history shows a prior kitchen-water claim from 2022. What is your first coverage action, and why?
questions.answer_key: Issue Reservation of Rights and call the insured to explain before the letter arrives. Rationale: 4A + panel-cause signature is present, but prior loss is unrelated cause family — still enough exposure through 4A dwelling extension to warrant ROR. Call before letter is a customer-preservation practice.

questions.prompt: The origin report comes back identifying the panel as a specific model on an active manufacturer recall list. Reserve is at $68,000. Walk through your next three actions in order.
questions.answer_key: 1) Open subrogation against the panel manufacturer. 2) Preserve the physical panel as evidence — physical chain of custody. 3) Release the ROR to the insured (same day, phone first) since origin cleared to a mechanical cause not a coverage issue.

questions.prompt: On day 4 of a similar file, the insured's attorney demands a six-week hotel commitment through ALE. Origin is still pending. How do you respond?
questions.answer_key: Decline the commitment while extending the current two-week hold. Explain to counsel that ALE is being managed on a rolling basis pending origin — not to punish the insured but because a six-week upfront commitment is unrecoverable if origin returns adverse. Offer to review at Day 10.

questions.prompt: A junior colleague asks: why bother preserving the panel on a small $12,000 fire when there's no 4A endorsement?
questions.answer_key: Because manufacturer recall subrogation is available regardless of endorsement. 4A affects dwelling extension exposure only, not the mechanical-cause subro path. Preserving the panel is cheap; burning the subro because you didn't is not.

questions.prompt: You reserve a panel-cause fire at $65,000 on Day 1. On Day 8 the fire marshal breaks the cause out as branch wiring, not the panel. What should you expect to happen to the reserve, and what does the playbook say to do?
questions.answer_key: Expect 30-40% upward revision to $85-90k. Wiring cases historically escalate. Adjust the reserve immediately upon confirmed origin; do not wait for Day 10 review.

micro_lessons.title: Reading a FNOL for panel-cause
micro_lessons.summary: Three language tells reliably signal panel-cause across Mike's 30+ handled claims. This lesson walks you through recognizing each one from raw FNOL text, with masked examples from actual files.

micro_lessons.title: The ROR-before-letter phone call
micro_lessons.summary: Mike's rule: never let a Reservation of Rights letter arrive cold. This lesson gives you the actual language he uses, the objections you'll hear, and how to close the call.

micro_lessons.title: Median-outcome reserving discipline
micro_lessons.summary: Why over-reserving hurts the book, the insured, and your own credibility. Median-outcome reserving with Day 10 / Day 30 review points — how Mike stays within 8% of final on 27 of the last 30 files.

micro_lessons.title: Preserve the panel — evidence chain 101
micro_lessons.summary: The single highest-leverage action on any panel-cause fire, regardless of file size. Chain of custody in five steps, what to photograph, and how to document.

micro_lessons.title: Cross-checking recall lists
micro_lessons.summary: The active recall databases, how to search by model, and what to file when you get a match.
